blocks.body: Present Perfect: Time Expressions — just, already, yet, ever, never, for, since, and other markers that connect the past with the present.

{"level":"A2–B1","intro":"Words like just, already, yet, ever, never, for, since often point to Present Perfect because they show a link between a past action and the present moment.","rules":[{"label":"just / already / yet","color":"emerald","text":"These words often talk about recent completion or an expected result by now.","example":"She has just arrived. / I have already eaten. / He hasn't called yet."},{"label":"ever / never","color":"blue","text":"These words often appear in questions and statements about life experience.","example":"Have you ever been to Spain? / We have never tried it."},{"label":"for / since","color":"amber","text":"They show a duration or a starting point that continues up to now.","example":"I have lived here for three years. / She has worked here since 2022."}]}

{"title":"1. Useful time expressions","intro":"Start with the most common Present Perfect markers.","items":[{"label":"Recent actions","title":"just / already / yet","subtitle":"just = a moment ago, already = before now, yet = still / already in questions and negatives."},{"label":"Experience","title":"ever / never","subtitle":"ever often appears in questions, and never in statements with a negative meaning."},{"label":"Duration","title":"for + period","subtitle":"for two hours / for a long time / for six months."},{"label":"Starting point","title":"since + point in time","subtitle":"since Monday / since 2020 / since breakfast."}]}

{"title":"2. How these words work","sections":[{"label":"Recent actions","color":"emerald","description":"Just and already often show an action completed close to the present moment.","examples":[{"en":"They have just arrived.","ua":"A very recent action."},{"en":"I have already done my homework.","ua":"The action is complete before now."}]},{"label":"Expected result","color":"blue","description":"Yet often appears in questions and negatives when we expect a result by now.","examples":[{"en":"Have you finished yet?","ua":"We expect a result by now."},{"en":"She hasn't called yet.","ua":"The result still has not happened."}]},{"label":"Life experience","color":"amber","description":"Ever and never help you talk about experience up to the present moment.","examples":[{"en":"Have you ever tried sushi?","ua":"Experience up to now."},{"en":"He has never flown alone.","ua":"No experience before now."}]},{"label":"Period up to now","color":"violet","description":"For gives a duration, and since gives the starting point of a period up to now.","examples":[{"en":"We have lived here for five years.","ua":"A duration up to now."},{"en":"She has studied English since September.","ua":"A starting point up to now."}]}]}

{"title":"3. Common combinations","intro":"See how time expressions work with Present Perfect.","rows":[{"en":"I have just finished lunch.","ua":"a recently completed action","note":"just"},{"en":"She has already seen this film.","ua":"the action is already complete by now","note":"already"},{"en":"Have they arrived yet?","ua":"an expected result by now","note":"yet"},{"en":"We have lived here since 2021.","ua":"a starting point up to now","note":"since"}],"warning":"The word ago usually goes with Past Simple, not with Present Perfect."}

{"title":"4. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Using ago with Present Perfect.","wrong":"❌ I have seen him two days ago.","right":"✅ I saw him two days ago."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Mixing up for and since.","wrong":"❌ She has lived here since three years.","right":"✅ She has lived here for three years."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using yet in the wrong sentence type or position.","wrong":"❌ I have yet finished the task.","right":"✅ I haven't finished the task yet."}]}

{"title":"5. Short summary","items":["just, already, and yet often show a result by the present moment.","ever and never are used for life experience.","for gives a duration, and since gives a starting point.","ago is usually not used with Present Perfect."]}
